channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Book Review: ‘Trash!: A Garbageman’s Story,’ by Simon Paré-Poupart
item.description: In his memoir, Simon Paré-Poupart recounts the highs and lows of hauling trash for more than 20 years.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner

item.title: Book Review: ‘Contrapposto,’ by Dave Eggers
item.description: In this satisfyingly old-school novel, an artist tries to find his place, and hold onto his spark, in a world that values fads and flash.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.media:credit: Poppy Lynch for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dave Eggers includes his own drawings in “Contrapposto,” his novel about artistic aspiration and compromise.

item.title: Sizzling New Summer Thrillers
item.description: Summer’s here. It’s hot. Let these books deliver some chills.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall

item.title: The Best Audiobooks of 2026 (So Far)
item.description: The year is nearly halfway over. Here’s what we’ve been listening to.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Christensen
item.category: A Hymn to Life: Shame Has to Change Sides (Book)
Days of Love and Rage: A Story of Ordinary People Forging a Revolution (Book)
London Falling: A Mysterious Death in a Gilded City and a Family's Search for Truth (Book)

item.title: Gordon S. Wood, Pioneering Historian of Early America, Dies at 92
item.description: In a Pulitzer-winning book, “The Radicalism of the American Revolution,” he wrote that the colonists rose up against an entire worldview, not just against taxation.
item.dc:creator: David Stout
item.category: The Radicalism of the American Revolution (Book)
Power and Liberty: Constitutionalism in the American Revolution (Book)
The 1619 Project: A New Origin Story (Book)
item.media:credit: Brooks Kraft LLC/Corbis, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Gordon S. Wood was awarded the National Humanities Medal by President Barack Obama in 2011. He worked to deepen understanding of the forces and events that led to the birth of the United States.

item.title: Robert Coles, Pulitzer-Winning Child Psychiatrist, Is Dead at 97
item.description: His five-volume “Children of Crisis” series, published between 1967 and 1977, drew on his conversations with American children whose voices were not often heard.
item.dc:creator: Douglas Martin
item.media:credit: Jodi Hilton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert Coles eschewed ideologies and psychiatric orthodoxies to visit the homes of children — first in the American South and then around the world — to listen intently to what they had to say.

item.title: Alan Riding, Times Correspondent in Latin America and Paris, Dies at 82
item.description: He was a cosmopolitan observer and interpreter of societies he knew firsthand, whether writing about war in Nicaragua or the history and cultural salons of France.
item.dc:creator: Alan Cowell
item.category: Distant Neighbors: A Portrait of the Mexicans (Book)
item.media:description: Alan Riding around 1980, when he was the Mexico City bureau chief of The New York Times, interviewing rebel leaders in El Salvador.

item.title: An Edith Wharton Short Story Is Published About 100 Years Later
item.description: The short story, which is set during World War I, is believed to have been printed for the first time on Friday. The story is thought to have been written no earlier than July 1918.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library
item.media:description: Edith Wharton in France in 1923. Famous for her sharp satires of New York society in the Gilded Age, Wharton spent World War I working on the front lines of French relief efforts.

item.title: In ‘Waist Deep,’ Linea Maja Ernst Explores Millennial Desire
item.description: The Danish writer Linea Maja Ernst’s debut novel, “Waist Deep,” a hit in Europe, explores the flirtations and frustrations within a millennial friendship circle.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: Mathias Eis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Linea Maja Ernst is polyamorous, but her wife isn’t. Although they love each other, they still haven’t quite “figured it out,” much like some of the characters in her debut novel.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Earth 7,’ by Deb Olin Unferth
item.description: Deb Olin Unferth’s new novel is part cosmic comedy and part dirge for our dying world.
item.dc:creator: Lincoln Michel

item.title: A Summer Book Recommendation Bonanza
item.description: From doppelgängers to dark academia, the Book Review editors share some of their most-anticipated titles.
item.dc:creator: Gilbert Cruz, Sarah Diamond, Amy Pearl, Patricia Sulbarán and Larissa Anderson
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; Inset covers, from left: via Doubleday; via Random House, via Harper

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Man Who Stole the Gods,’ by Matthew Campbell
item.description: In “The Man Who Stole the Gods,” Matthew Campbell recounts how cultural artifacts were pillaged in Cambodia.
item.dc:creator: Hugh Eakin
item.category: The Man Who Stole the Gods: A True Story of War, Obsession, and a Global Art Conspiracy (Book)
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon for The New York Times
item.media:description: In the fallout from the Cambodian civil war, opportunistic thieves looted and dealt in a wealth of antiquities, like this 10th-century sandstone sculpture, to an avid Western market.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Villa Coco,’ by Andrew Sean Greer
item.description: Following his “Less” books with “Villa Coco,” Andrew Sean Greer drops an aimless postgraduate into a glamorous, romantic and secret-laden setting.
item.dc:creator: Stuart Emmrich

item.title: ‘The Story of Ferdinand,’ by Munro Leaf and Robert Lawson, Turns 90
item.description: As the gentle giant who just wanted to live his best life turns 90, Munro Leaf and Robert Lawson’s classic fable is as apt as ever.
item.dc:creator: Sadie Stein
item.category: Carle, Eric, Museum of Picture Book Art

item.title: Books Our Editors Loved This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.
item.category: The Man Who Read Everything: The Literary Letters of Harold Bloom (Book)

item.title: Alan Gribben, Twain Scholar Who Excised Slur From ‘Huck Finn,’ Dies at 84
item.description: He made it his mission to track down every book Mark Twain owned — and to fix what he saw as flaws that kept schools from teaching the author’s most famous works.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: Book Review: ‘Checkmate,’ by Ben Mezrich
item.description: The story at the heart of “Checkmate” is a good one, but Ben Mezrich has hustled it into print too quickly.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Loeb McClain
item.category: Checkmate: Genius, Lies, Ambition, and the Biggest Scandal in Chess (Book)
item.media:description: Norway’s Magnus Carlsen, pictured here, made waves in the chess world when he accused Hans Niemann, a brash young American, of cheating.

item.title: 11 Books That Capture the Swoonworthy Drama of Wedding Season
item.description: Some will have you mentally arranging flowers for your own happy day. Others provide the vicarious thrill of watching it all burn.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Singer

item.title: Caissie Levy, Tony Awards Contender and ‘Ragtime’ Star, Is Having a Moment
item.description: Caissie Levy was Broadway’s first Elsa. She starred in “Hair” and “Ghost.” And now, for “Ragtime,” she is an odds-on favorite to win a Tony Award.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and George Etheredge
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Traveler,’ by Andrea Wulf
item.description: As presented in Andrea Wulf’s new biography, “The Traveler,” George Forster was an impressively curious, open-minded 17-year-old naturalist and polymath.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.category: The Traveler: One Man's Quest for Humanity From the South Seas to Revolutionary Paris (Book)

item.title: Carley Fortune Keeps Writing Hit Romance Novels
item.description: Carley Fortune left a hard-won journalism job to give fiction a shot. Five best-sellers later, a series based on her debut is about to stream.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Egan
item.media:credit: Narisa Ladak for The New York Times

item.title: How Poets Went From Describing Art to Personally Admiring It
item.description: The form known as ekphrasis — or poetry about art — has taken a turn toward the individual. Our columnist asks what it means.
item.dc:creator: Elisa Gabbert

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Wreck of the Mentor,’ by Eric Jay Dolin
item.description: In “The Wreck of the Mentor,” the maritime historian Eric Jay Dolin brings to life a dramatic episode from the golden age of whaling.
item.dc:creator: Blair Braverman
item.category: The Wreck of the Mentor: A True Story of Death, Despair, and Deliverance in the Age of Sail (Book)

item.title: In Her Memoir, Jill Biden Is a Watchful Spouse Who Didn’t Always Speak Up About Joe Biden
item.description: The former first lady’s new book reflects an insular White House where loyalty was prized and President Joseph R. Biden Jr.’s feelings were prioritized over health concerns.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.category: View from the East Wing: A Memoir (Book)
item.media:description: Jill Biden in 2022. In her telling, by the time she got a glimpse of what others had feared about her husband as president, it was too late.

item.title: Book Review: ‘What Came West,’ by Josh Weil
item.description: Josh Weil’s new novel follows an autistic trapper on an odyssey during the California gold rush.
item.dc:creator: Hamilton Cain

item.title: Book Review: ‘Land,’ by Maggie O’Farrell
item.description: Set in the decades after the Great Hunger, “Land” is a rich portrait of family life amid Ireland’s long struggle against British rule.
item.dc:creator: Fiona Mozley

item.title: Book Review: ‘Stolen Revolution,’ by Yeganeh Torbati and Bozorgmehr Sharafedin
item.description: In a quietly devastating new book, two journalists chart the protest movements fighting for change inside the country.
item.dc:creator: Reza Aslan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Stolen Revolution: Betrayal and Hope in Modern Iran (Book)
item.media:description: Iranians gather on Tehran’s Shah Reza Avenue, now called Enqelab (“Revolution”) Street, in 1979.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Fire Agent,’ by David Baerwald
item.description: “The Fire Agent,” by David Baerwald, is a historical novel that spans two continents and world wars.
item.dc:creator: Dan Fesperman
